narrative: As a Black male student applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, LSAT score of 34, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 3rd, and middle family income bracket, along with planning to attend full-time. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 55.5% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on just a few key aspects of your profile compared to typical applicants. Your LSAT score was the single biggest factor pulling the prediction toward success, as it stood strong enough to signal solid potential despite being a bit below the average of around 37. However, your 3rd-decile ranking in Year 3 grades had the second-strongest influence, pushing the model toward predicting failure because it was notably lower than the typical 5th or 6th decile for admitted students. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 3rd reinforced that concern, as early academic performance like yours often correlates with bar struggles in the model's view of past students. These grade deciles outweighed even your LSAT's positive sway, tipping the balance against you when stacked against peers who usually rank higher in school performance. While your middle income and full-time plans align with most applicants and didn't sway things much, the model saw your overall academic deciles as red flags based on historical patterns for students with similar backgrounds. It's tough to hear, especially knowing the extra hurdles many Black men face in competitive fields like law, but this decision reflects data trends, not your full potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening your recent grades through post-bac courses or targeted prep to shift those deciles. Many students in your position have succeeded by addressing these exact areas before reapplying—reach out to advisors for bar prep resources tailored to your profile, and explore schools with holistic reviews that weigh personal stories alongside stats. With focused effort, you can rewrite this prediction on your next application.
